topic: A person finds out they were adopted through a DNA test
raw_output:
[story 1]

The printout lay on the kitchen counter, stark white against the granite. My hands were shaking as I traced the lines of the genetic report. It wasn't just a confirmation; it was a complete remapping of my origins. Every sequence, every marker, pointed to a family I’d never known, a lineage that felt impossibly distant yet undeniably real. I pushed the paper away, the sound of the plastic rustling loud in the silence of the apartment. My breath hitched, a shallow, rapid thing in my chest. I started pacing the small space, my heels clicking a frantic rhythm against the hardwood floor. Looking at my reflection in the darkened window, I searched for something familiar, but the face staring back seemed alien, a stranger wearing my skin.

[story 2]

The results arrived tucked inside a plain envelope, the kind you expect for official correspondence. Opening it felt like cracking open a sealed vault. When my eyes scanned the comparative data, a cold dread began to pool in my stomach. The percentages aligned, the matches undeniable. It wasn't a surprise, exactly; it was the devastating realization that the narrative I’d built for myself was fundamentally incomplete. I sat down heavily on the edge of the sofa, my fingers gripping the fabric until my knuckles were white. A strange pressure built behind my eyes, a dull ache that made focusing on anything else impossible. I kept running my thumb over the edge of the paper, a futile attempt to smooth out the jagged edges of this new, unwelcome truth.

[story 3]

I stared at the screen, the complex biological diagrams blurring into meaningless noise. The confirmation blinked in stark blue letters, a verdict delivered without preamble. A wave of nausea washed over me, making the room tilt slightly. I stood up abruptly, needing to move, needing distance from the evidence. My movements were jerky, inefficient. I walked to the balcony, leaning against the cool railing, trying to inhale deeply, but the air felt thin, insufficient. Every sound—the distant traffic, the hum of the refrigerator—seemed amplified, pressing in on me. I squeezed my eyes shut, trying to force the images, the possibilities, to retreat, but they remained, vivid and intrusive.
